data.title: US Foods Holding: Visible Growth Story With Optionality (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Summary

Following my coverage on US Foods Holding (NYSE:USFD), for which I recommended a hold rating as I felt the growth expectations for FY25 were too much, especially with regard to independent restaurant case volumes, this post is to provide an update on my thoughts on the business and stock. I now see tangible evidence of a real inflection. Independent case volume growth has accelerated meaningfully, and margin expansion levers are working. Combined with the optionality of a potential merger with Performance Food Group, USFD stock looks far better than before. I upgrade to buy.

From "Show-Me" Story to Proven Execution

As I said above, the decision for me to downgrade to a hold rating was due to my skepticism around management’s ambitious FY25 growth assumptions, particularly the 4–7% case growth target for independent restaurants. At the time, it looked more like a “show-me” story than a credible outlook, especially given the soft 1.8% growth seen in 4Q24. The risk was real, in that if execution fell short, a multiple de-rating was likely to happen. However, the good news is that I was wrong, and that what once looked like an implausible goal now seems very much in reach.

There is tangible evidence to back this claim. Firstly, organic independent case volume growth saw a very positive inflection, accelerating meaningfully to 2.3% in 2Q25 (from 1.8% in 1Q25). Secondly, management commentary adds more conviction here. They specifically noted that growth reached ~3% in June, held steady through July, and showed further sequential improvement in August. This implies August volume growth to be around 4%, which seems fair given that USFD saw its strongest net new account growth since 4Q23 in the recent quarter, which came in at ~4% y/y. Together, these datapoints provide forward-looking visibility that de-risks the 2H25 ramp embedded in guidance—suppose momentum continues, say by ~200 bps per quarter, USFD would end 3Q25 with ~400 bps volume growth and 600 bps volume growth in 4Q25, blending out to low-400 bps volume growth on a full-year basis (in line with guidance).

Self-help initiatives

The margin story is even more powerful. While I had previously acknowledged USFD’s internal levers as a potential driver for margin expansion, it was great to see these levers materializing into actual margin improvements. For numerical references, adj. gross profit per case grew 4% y/y in 2Q25, much better than the 1.6% y/y increase in adj. operating expense per case, which resulted in an 11% y/y lift in adj. EBITDA per case. Looking ahead, I expect more expansion here, as the 2 main key drivers are still in place:

  1. 1. Vendor management has progressed extremely well, with savings exceeding $50 million year-to-date and management guiding for >$110 million savings for FY25 and a line-of-sight to surpass the original $260 million 2027 goal.
  2. 2. The Pronto small-truck delivery, a delivery service that utilizes smaller box trucks to supplement USFD's main fleet of larger vehicles, should continue to drive margin expansion. There are two parts here: "Pronto Legacy" and "Pronto Penetration." For Pronto Legacy, it targets new customers, specifically those in dense geographic areas that are difficult for large trucks to service or operators with smaller storage spaces who require more frequent, smaller deliveries. This part of the program has been in the market for several years. The focus is on the newer and rapidly growing component, Pronto Penetration, which is focused on increasing share-of-wallet with existing broadline customers. It allows these customers to place smaller, fill-in orders between their regularly scheduled large deliveries, which are then fulfilled by the more nimble Pronto trucks. In other words, this helps USFD to maximize capacity by capturing business that might otherwise go to smaller specialty suppliers, and this drives operating leverage. In fact, demand has been so good that it pushed management to raise its 2027 sales target by 50% to $1.5 billion.

Other growth and margin drivers

USFD's long-term competition position also looks better. In my prior update, I mentioned the importance of digital tools, and fast forward to today, we continue to see USFD advancing in this aspect. The MOXE e-commerce platform is now recognized as best-in-class, and USFD has achieved the highest customer digital adoption in the industry (source: 2Q25 earnings call). For those unaware, MOXE is USFD's all-in-one digital ordering and account management platform designed for its customers. It allows restaurant operators to handle everything from placing orders and tracking deliveries in real-time to managing inventory and paying invoices. It is important because it makes USFD a stickier and more efficient partner. For customers, it simplifies the complexities of running a kitchen and provides valuable data and insights. For USFD, it helps to increase customer loyalty, drives larger order sizes, and makes customers more profitable. It also frees up the sales team from manual order-taking, allowing them to focus on higher-value activities like consulting and relationship-building. Clearly, the value proposition is resonating with customers, and we can see this playing out in the numbers as independent e-commerce penetration grew to a record 78%, which I expect to continue going up. On top of that, USFD is deploying its first semi-automated warehouse in Illinois, with a Texas facility to follow. This should help to unlock better supply chain efficiency, which drives margin expansion.

Potential PFGC merger

Finally, there is a near-term catalyst to look forward to—a potential merger with Performance Food Group. My view here is straightforward: this combination would create a stronger competitor with meaningful economies of scale. Consolidating distribution networks and increasing supplier leverage could materially reduce costs, while the combined platform would offer customers a broader product range and enhanced service. For shareholders, I believe this is appealing because of the potential for multi-year synergies and profitable growth. The key variable, of course, is regulatory approval. That said, even the initiation of this process adds a compelling layer of optionality to the investment case.

Valuation

Although the share price has run up quite a bit and valuation (forward EBITDA) has also gone up to 11x today, I think there is still upside to capture here. I believe revenue growth should accelerate given the improving volume growth momentum and also the self-help initiatives. Margins should expand accordingly as USFD scales (with the self-help initiatives also boosting margin). I did not include in my model the merger with Performance Food Group since this is arbitrary. But even without that, I think USFD can deliver ~$2.5 billion on EBITDA in FY28. At 11x forward EBITDA, which I think is fair considering the improving growth and higher margin, this implies USFD shares to be worth $101 in FY27, or a 31% upside.

Risk

Key risks are still with the macroeconomic environment. A slowdown in consumer discretionary spending could soften restaurant traffic, directly pressuring top-line growth. While self-help initiatives have been successful, USFD is inherently exposed to product cost swings (especially raw ingredient prices), which may swing at a much larger magnitude than expected. Finally, uncertainty surrounding the potential merger with Performance Food Group can also be seen as an overhang since some market participants may wait for this event to clear before getting a position in the stock.

Conclusion

In my view, USFD has transitioned from a “show-me” story into one of proven execution. With accelerating volume growth, margin continuing to expand, and self-help initiatives, I think USFD has de-risked its near-term guidance. The added optionality of a potential merger with Performance Food Group also adds to the bull case.

data.title: 4 Scenarios For Fannie Mae And Its Preferreds
data.description:
With the Trump administration's recent hints, a public offering of shares in mortgage guarantors Fannie Mae (OTCQB:FNMA) and Freddie Mac (OTCQB:FMCC) could occur this year, a resolution to the question we've had since last year appears imminent--which is better to own, common stock or various issues of junior preferreds?

The answer mainly depends on two factors--the offering price of the common, and the details of how the preferreds are handled. There are scenarios that favor the common, favor the preferreds, or are good for certain preferreds but bad for others.

For simplicity's sake, this article will focus only on Fannie, the larger and more heavily traded of the two government-sponsored enterprises that have been in conservatorship since 2008. A similar analysis could be made for Freddy.

Let's look at four of the main scenarios:

1) Government's Senior Preferred Stock Purchase Agreements (SPSPAs) are deemed repaid. Junior preferred holders receive a rights offering allowing them to convert to common shares at par.

Cancellation of the SPSPAs is the scenario envisioned by hedge fund manager Bill Ackman's Pershing Square Capital Management in their presentation valuing FNMA at $35 by the end of 2026, with an IPO price of $31.81 at that time.

Common shareholders, including Ackman, argue that the government has been more than repaid with 10% interest for the $121 billion in loans it provided Fannie. If the Trump administration agrees, shareholders could see a double or better from the recent price.

For preferred holders, a rights offering allowing them to convert to common could mean they would get face value in the form of common shares, subject to a cap on the amounts that could be converted.

This is good for the $19 billion in legacy preferreds issued before 2008, especially the ones that are selling at a lower percentage of face value, such as FNMAH, which recently sold at 48% of face.

The high-coupon, liquid preferred issues FNMAS and FNMAT would rise less, since they are already at about 65-67% of face.

To help investors shop for the best buys, this Google spreadsheet lists the preferreds in order of lowest percentage of face value.

Here is a simplified chart of the projected returns with IPO prices of $10 (the B. Riley price target), $20 (the Deutsche Bank price target), and $30 (close to the Pershing Square valuation).

Projected returns with full conversion of preferreds to common at face
Issue Recent price IPO=$10 IPO=$20 IPO=$30
FNMA 13 -23% 54% 131%
FNMAT 15.86 58% 58% 58%
FNMAS 16.7 50% 50% 50%
FNMAH 13.28 88% 88% 88%

Source: Author's spreadsheet

As you can see, preferreds would be in line for big gains no matter what the offering price for FNMA. The common provides more bounce if the IPO is at $30 or more but falls if it is at $10.

A variant of this scenario would occur if the preferreds were subject to mandatory conversion to common. This would produce similar results, but could be subject to legal challenges that would delay the offering.

2) SPSPs are left intact. Junior preferred holders get conversion rights.

Let's call this the Clint Eastwood scenario, since the senior preferreds would be unforgiven. This is about the same as Scenario 1 for junior preferred holders but much less beneficial for common holders, since they would suffer significantly more dilution from eventual conversion of the SPSPs to common stock in addition to the dilution they already expect from conversion of the government's 79.9% stake in warrants. This scenario is closest to the opinions on Seeking Alpha by Glen Bradford and Noah's Arc Capital Management.

All series of preferreds would likely significantly outperform the common.

I agree with B. Riley's analysis that this scenario is less likely.

Riley identifies two potential paths forward: the government could either maximize its senior position held by the U.S. Treasury and require recapitalization, which would be highly dilutive to current equity holders, or forgive the Treasury’s $121 billion senior preferred position.

The firm interprets recent comments by Treasury Secretary Bessent as aligning with the second scenario, which would minimize up-front dilution and maximize government sale of FNMA shares to investors."

Let's move on to Scenarios 3 and 4, which refer only to the junior preferreds and could be done with either treatment of the SPSPAs.

3) The offering document for common shares indicates dividends will be reinstated immediately for junior preferreds.

Turning the dividends back on after 17 years would scramble the valuation of the junior preferreds. With high coupons, FNMAS (with a dividend at face of 8.5%) and FNMAT (8.25%) likely would shoot up to par by the time of dividend reinstatement and probably a little higher, capped by the fact they are subject to being called at $25 at any time.

Low-paying issues like FNMAP (coupon rate 3.41%) would rise much less since their dividend rate would be well below market for junior preferreds, although it might still rise a bit since the yield at the recent price (7.2%) would be attractive.

I don't see this as too likely, as the government probably would prefer building capital to having to pay out cash immediately to pay high dividends or call FNMAS and FNMAT, which have $9 billion of face value, about half of all of the preferreds.

4) Offering includes no action on junior preferred, which are left in limbo.

This is the worst scenario for the preferreds--dividends might not be reinstated until they legally would have to be so the common dividend could also be reinstated. That could take a few years as the enterprise continues to build capital to meet reserve requirements.

Non-paying, non-cumulative preferreds tend to sell at a significant discount to par until payments are reinstated.

Eventually, what would probably happen is that the high-rate preferreds would be called as soon as dividends were turned back on because Fannie wouldn't want to pay above-market rates, while lower-rate ones would be left to trudge on with prices below face value, disappointing shareholders.

Thus, while the high-rate ones like FNMAS and FNMAT would be the better place to be, none of the preferreds would rise much immediately, and some, like the aforementioned FNMAP might fall significantly.

Risk Factors

A major risk is that for some reason, including lawsuits, the public offering does not occur in a timely manner. One possible source of delay is a merger of two GSEs as suggested by President Trump.

Regulations mandating high capital reserves could prevent the payment of dividends for several years, reducing investor interest in buying both the common and preferreds.

As discussed, the risk of the government's retention of its SPSP rights applies mainly to the common.

Conclusion

My guess is Scenario 1 is most likely based on the Trump administration's desire to drum up enthusiasm for the offering, but wanting to be positioned for any of them.

I hold FNMA common, a high-coupon preferred (OTCQB:FNMAT), and two medium-coupon preferreds (FNMAH and FNMFN). This diversifies risk and opportunity among the various scenarios. I am staying away from low-coupon preferreds like FNMAP because of the risk of Scenarios 3 and 4.

Taken as a whole, I expect the group to do well in any of these cases as long as the offering takes place before the midterm elections in November 2026. Once a registration statement is filed with the Securities and Exchange Commission, I will probably adjust my positions to reflect more certain knowledge.
